description:
The skill doc's "Multi-repo: one project, many repos" section covers the
mechanics (state_remote knob, bl remaster onboarding, --detach to leave)
but leaves the day-to-day operational model unspecified. Agents working
in a multi-repo setup have to guess at:

- Which code repo a given ball "belongs to" when N code repos share one
  task hub (no tag convention is suggested; no `repo` field exists).
- Where bl claim places its worktree when the task's real code lives in
  a sibling repo — claim creates under the current clone's
  .balls-worktrees/, so claiming from the "wrong" clone silently puts
  the worktree in the wrong place.
- What the hub itself is (a bare repo holding just balls/tasks? a real
  code repo doubling as the hub? both are valid; the doc doesn't say).
- Whether `bl prime` in one participant shows tasks scoped to that
  repo or every ball on the shared branch.
- How a task that requires changes in two code repos is modeled — one
  ball or two linked balls.

Beyond filling those gaps, document the **tracker-proxy pattern**
explicitly: a multi-repo project frequently wants one external tracker
(Jira, Linear, GitHub Issues) as the single source of truth for
*humans*. The natural fit with balls is to wire the plugin (e.g.
github-issues) into exactly one participating repo — the bridge — and
let the other code repos operate through the shared state branch as a
proxy. They never need plugin credentials or hooks of their own; the
bridge syncs in both directions, and a ball filed from any repo
surfaces on the external tracker via the bridge's next sync.

Why: this is the lowest-coordination way to keep external visibility
without N copies of the plugin config (and N race conditions). It also
keeps the policy decision in one place — only one repo's plugin
controls what gets mirrored outward.

Deliverable: extend the Multi-repo section to:
1. Suggest a tag-or-prefix convention for "which repo" (or
   acknowledge none is enforced and recommend one).
2. State the rule: claim from the clone whose code the ball touches;
   the worktree lands under that clone.
3. Describe the bridge / proxy pattern with a concrete example
   (github-issues plugin enabled in repo A; repos B and C share the
   hub; a ball filed from B appears as a GH issue via A's next sync).
4. Clarify discovery scope (bl prime shows the whole shared branch).
5. Note cross-cutting tasks: prefer one ball with a tag listing each
   repo, or one umbrella parent with per-repo children.
title: Document multi-repo operational model and tracker-proxy pattern
